devanagari: त्रिशंकुकथा हरिश्चंद्रचरितं च । श्रीशुक उवाच । मान्धातुः पुत्रप्रवरो योऽम्बरीषः प्रकीर्तितः । पितामहेन प्रवृतो यौवनाश्वश्च तत्सुतः । हारी...
english_devnagari: triśaṃkukathā hariścaṃdracaritaṃ ca śrīśuka uvāca māndhātuḥ putrapravaro yo pitāmahena pravṛto yauvanāśvaśca tatsutaḥ hārītastasya putro

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
The celebrated Ambarīṣa, who was the most excellent son of Māndhātṛ, was adopted as his??n [son?] by his grandfather (Yuvanāśva). His (Ambarīṣa’s) son was Yauvanāśva whose son was Hārīta. These are the three important members (and the first three branches of the Māndhātṛ family).

devanagari: नर्मदा भ्रातृभिर्दत्ता पुरुकुत्साय योरगैः । तया रसातलं नीतो भुजगेन्द्रप्रयुक्तया ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: narmadā bhrātṛbhirdattā purukutsāya yoragaiḥ tayā rasātalaṃ nīto bhujagendraprayuktayā
translation: Narmadā (a Nāga princess) was given by her Nāga brothers to Purukutsa (the eldest son of Māndhātṛ). As directed by the serpent-King (Vāsuki), she took him (Purukutsa) to the nether-world called Rasātala.

devanagari: गन्धर्वान् अवधीत् तत्र वध्यान् वै विष्णुशक्तिधृक् । नागाल्लब्धवरः सर्पात् अभयं स्मरतामिदम् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: gandharvān avadhīt tatra vadhyān vai viṣṇuśaktidhṛk nāgāllabdhavaraḥ sarpāt abhayaṃ smaratāmidam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: Infused with the spiritual energy of god Viṣṇu, Purukutsa killed Gandharvas who deserved to be slain. He received a boon from the Nāgas that those who remember this story (of emancipation of Nāgas from Gandharvas) would be immune from the fear of serpents.

devanagari: त्रसद्दस्युः पौरुकुत्सो योऽनरण्यस्य देहकृत् । हर्यश्वः तत्सुतः तस्मात् अरुणोऽथ त्रिबन्धनः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: trasaddasyuḥ paurukutso yo haryaśvaḥ tatsutaḥ tasmāt aruṇo
translation: Purukutsa’s son was Trasaddasyu (the second[1]) who was the father of Anaraṇya. His son was Haryaśva whose son was Aruṇa. Aruṇa’s son was Tribandhana.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
‘trasad-dasyu’ is the name of Māndhātṛ also. Vide supra. 9.6.33

devanagari: तस्य सत्यव्रतः पुत्रः त्रिशङ्कुरिति विश्रुतः । प्राप्तश्चाण्डालतां शापाग् गुरोः कौशिकतेजसा ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya satyavrataḥ putraḥ triśaṅkuriti viśrutaḥ prāptaścāṇḍālatāṃ śāpāg guroḥ kauśikatejasā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]

translation: Tribandhana’s son Satyavrata became celebrated as Triśaṅku.[2] He was reduced to the status of a Cāṇḍāla by the curse of his father, but by dint of the spiritual power of Viśvāmitra, he ascended heaven along with his (physical) body, where he is still seen in the sky. When he was made to fall with his head downward by gods, he (Viśvāmitra) stopped him in the mid-air by the spiritual force (of Viśvāmitra).
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā explains: He is called Triśaṅku—one in whom are embedded three darts in the form of three offences, viz. abduction of a Brāhmaṇa girl from her marriage pandal, killing the cow of his preceptor Vasiṣṭha, eating meat before its consecration with water. He quotes a verse from the Harivaṃśa enumerating these transgressions:
pituṣ cā'paritoṣeṇa gururdogdhrīvadhena ca /
aprokṣitopayogācca trividhas te vyatikramaḥ //

devanagari: सशरीरो गतः स्वर्गं अद्यापि दिवि दृश्यते । पातितोऽवाक्शिरा देवैः तेनैव स्तम्भितो बलात् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: saśarīro gataḥ svargaṃ adyāpi divi dṛśyate pātito
translation: Tribandhana’s son Satyavrata became celebrated as Triśaṅku.[2] He was reduced to the status of a Cāṇḍāla by the curse of his father, but by dint of the spiritual power of Viśvāmitra, he ascended heaven along with his (physical) body, where he is still seen in the sky. When he was made to fall with his head downward by gods, he (Viśvāmitra) stopped him in the mid-air by the spiritual force (of Viśvāmitra).
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā explains: He is called Triśaṅku—one in whom are embedded three darts in the form of three offences, viz. abduction of a Brāhmaṇa girl from her marriage pandal, killing the cow of his preceptor Vasiṣṭha, eating meat before its consecration with water. He quotes a verse from the Harivaṃśa enumerating these transgressions:
pituṣ cā'paritoṣeṇa gururdogdhrīvadhena ca /
aprokṣitopayogācca trividhas te vyatikramaḥ //

devanagari: त्रैशङ्कवो हरिश्चन्द्रो विश्वामित्रवसिष्ठयोः । यन्निमित्तमभूद् युद्धं पक्षिणोर्बहुवार्षिकम् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: traiśaṅkavo hariścandro viśvāmitravasiṣṭhayoḥ yannimittamabhūd yuddhaṃ pakṣiṇorbahuvārṣikam

translation: Triśaṅku’s son was Hariścandra on account of whom[3] a continuous fight lasting for many years took place between Vasiṣṭha and Viśvāmitra in the form of birds (viz. a crane (āḍī, Viśvāmitra) and a heron (Vasiṣṭha).
purport:
Footnote [3]:
This refers to the persecution of Hariścandra by the sage Viśvāmitra who deprived Hariścandra of everything as a sacrificial fee for conducting a Rājasūya sacrifice. When Vasiṣṭha learnt of this, he cursed Viśvāmitra to be a crane (āḍi) while Viśvāmitra retaliated by imprecating him to be a heron (baka). They fought for yean in these forms—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā

devanagari: सोऽनपत्यो विषण्णात्मा नारदस्योपदेशतः । वरुणं शरणं यातः पुत्रो मे जायतां प्रभो ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: so varuṇaṃ śaraṇaṃ yātaḥ putro me jāyatāṃ prabho

translation: He (Hariścandra) being issueless became dejected in mind. On the advice of Nārada, he resorted to god Varuṇa with the request, “Oh Varuṇa, Bless me with a son.

devanagari: यदि वीरो महाराज तेनैव त्वां यजे इति । तथेति वरुणेनास्य पुत्रो जातस्तु रोहितः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: yadi vīro mahārāja tenaiva tvāṃ yaje iti tatheti varuṇenāsya putro jātastu rohitaḥ

translation: “If he grows to be a warrior, I shall worship you by sacrificing him to you”. ‘Be it so’, replied Varuṇa in granting him the boon. And a son called Rohita was born to him, through the grace of Varuṇa.

devanagari: जातः सुतो ह्यनेनाङ्ग मां यजस्वेति सोऽब्रवीत् । यदा पशुर्निर्दशः स्याद् अथ मेध्यो भवेदिति ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: jātaḥ suto hyanenāṅga māṃ yajasveti so yadā paśurnirdaśaḥ syād atha medhyo bhavediti

translation: Varuṇa demanded, “Now a son is born to you, worship me by sacrificing that child”. Hariścandra replied, “When a (prospective) victim crosses the first ten days of his life, it becomes fit for sacrificial purpose.”

devanagari: निर्दशे च स आगत्य यजस्वेत्याह सोऽब्रवीत् । दन्ताः पशोर्यत् जायेरन् अथ मेध्यो भवेदिति ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: nirdaśe ca sa āgatya yajasvetyāha so dantāḥ paśoryat jāyeran atha medhyo bhavediti

translation: When the time limit of ten days was over, Varuṇa arrived and asked the king to worship him (with that child). Hariścandra replied, “It is after dentition that an animal becomes fit for sacrifice.”

devanagari: दन्ता जाता यजस्वेति स प्रत्याहाथ सोऽब्रवीत् । यदा पतन्त्यस्य दन्ता अथ मेध्यो भवेदिति ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: dantā jātā yajasveti sa pratyāhātha so yadā patantyasya dantā atha medhyo bhavediti

translation: “(After the dentition of the child, Varuṇa appeared again and) said, “Now that the teeth have appeared, do you worship me with the child?” “But the victim will be fit for sacrifice when its teeth have fallen.”

devanagari: पशोर्निपतिता दन्ता यजस्वेत्याह सोऽब्रवीत् । यदा पशोः पुनर्दन्ता जायन्तेऽथ पशुः शुचिः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: paśornipatitā dantā yajasvetyāha so yadā paśoḥ punardantā jāyante
translation: (Varuṇa appeared again and (demanded, “The teeth of the sacrificial animal have fallen, now worship (me with him)”. “But a victim becomes pure when its teeth grow again.” replied Hariścandra.

devanagari: पुनर्जाता यजस्वेति स प्रत्याहाथ सोऽब्रवीत् । सान्नाहिको यदा राजन् राजन्योऽथ पशुः शुचिः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: punarjātā yajasveti sa pratyāhātha so sānnāhiko yadā rājan rājanyo

translation: “Now that (the teeth) have grown again, sacrifice it now to me”, said Varuṇa. To him replied Hariścandra, “Oh King! When a Kṣatriya victim is (of the age of) wearing an armour, he is regarded as a pure animal fit for sacrifice.”

devanagari: इति पुत्रानुरागेण स्नेहयन् त्रितचेतसा । कालं वञ्चयता तं तमुक्तो देवस्तमैक्षत ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: iti putrānurāgeṇa snehayan tritacetasā kālaṃ vañcayatā taṃ tamukto devastamaikṣata

translation: Having come to learn that his father is possessed by Varuṇa and has developed mahodara (a disease called ‘Dropsy’), Rohita was returning to the capital when Indra forbade him to do so (lest he should be sacrificed).

devanagari: भूमेः पर्यटनं पुण्यं तीर्थक्षेत्रनिषेवणैः । रोहितायादिशच्छक्रः सोऽप्यरण्येऽवसत् समाम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: bhūmeḥ paryaṭanaṃ puṇyaṃ tīrthakṣetraniṣevaṇaiḥ rohitāyādiśacchakraḥ so

translation: Indra advised Rohita to travel all over the earth, as visiting the holy places and ablutions in sacred waters are meritorious, and Rohita stayed in the forest that year.

devanagari: एवं द्वितीये तृतीये चतुर्थे पञ्चमे तथा । अभ्येत्याभ्येत्य स्थविरो विप्रो भूत्वाऽह वृत्रहा ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ dvitīye tṛtīye caturthe pañcame tathā abhyetyābhyetya sthaviro vipro bhūtvā
translation: Assuming the form of an old Brāhmaṇa, Indra visited him in the second, third, fourth and fifth year and tendered him the same advice.

devanagari: षष्ठं संवत्सरं तत्र चरित्वा रोहितः पुरीम् । उपव्रजन् अजीगर्ताद् अक्रीणान् मध्यमं सुतम् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: ṣaṣṭhaṃ saṃvatsaraṃ tatra caritvā rohitaḥ purīm upavrajan ajīgartād akrīṇān madhyamaṃ sutam

translation: Having wandered in the forest for the sixth year, Rohita returned to his capital (and on way) purchased the middle son (the second son by name Śunaḥśepa) from Ajīgarta (of the Bhṛgu clan).

devanagari: शुनःशेपं पशुं पित्रे प्रदाय समवन्दत । ततः पुरुषमेधेन हरिश्चन्द्रो महायशाः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: śunaḥśepaṃ paśuṃ pitre pradāya samavandata tataḥ puruṣamedhena hariścandro mahāyaśāḥ
translation: Handing over Śunaḥśepa as a sacrificial animal to his father, Rohita bowed down to him. King Hariścandra of great glory, worshipped Varuṇa and other gods with human sacrifice and became free from dropsy, and his stories came to be sung in the assemblies of the great. In that sacrifice Viśvāmitra was the high priest (hotṛ), the self-controlled (or one who has realized the self), Jamadagni officiated as adhvaryu, Vasiṣṭha became the Brahmā, and the sage Ayāsya sang the Sāman hymns. Being pleased with him, Indra gifted to him a gold chariot.

devanagari: मुक्तोदरोऽयजद् देवान् वरुणादीन् महत्कथः । विश्वामित्रोऽभवत् तस्मिन् होता चाध्वर्युरात्मवान् ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: muktodaro viśvāmitro
translation: Handing over Śunaḥśepa as a sacrificial animal to his father, Rohita bowed down to him. King Hariścandra of great glory, worshipped Varuṇa and other gods with human sacrifice and became free from dropsy, and his stories came to be sung in the assemblies of the great. In that sacrifice Viśvāmitra was the high priest (hotṛ), the self-controlled (or one who has realized the self), Jamadagni officiated as adhvaryu, Vasiṣṭha became the Brahmā, and the sage Ayāsya sang the Sāman hymns. Being pleased with him, Indra gifted to him a gold chariot.

devanagari: जमदग्निरभूद् ब्रह्मा वसिष्ठोऽयास्यः सामगः । तस्मै तुष्टो ददौ इन्द्रः शातकौम्भमयं रथम् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: jamadagnirabhūd brahmā vasiṣṭho tasmai tuṣṭo dadau indraḥ śātakaumbhamayaṃ ratham

translation:
Handing over Śunaḥśepa as a sacrificial animal to his father, Rohita bowed down to him. King Hariścandra of great glory, worshipped Varuṇa and other gods with human sacrifice and became free from dropsy, and his stories came to be sung in the assemblies of the great. In that sacrifice Viśvāmitra was the high priest (hotṛ), the self-controlled (or one who has realized the self), Jamadagni officiated as adhvaryu, Vasiṣṭha became the Brahmā, and the sage Ayāsya sang the Sāman hymns. Being pleased with him, Indra gifted to him a gold chariot.
24-25(A). The greatness of Śunaḥśepa will be recounted later on (in ch. 16). Being highly pleased to notice the resoluteness deriving its strength from firmness in truth of both the king and the queen, he (Viśvāmitra) imparted to them the unobstructed knowledge of the ultimate truth.
25(B)-27[4]. (In meditation) fusing his mind (the root-cause of Saṃsāra[5]) into the earth (which is another designation for food which constitutes and hence modifies the mind[6]), the earth into water, water into fire, fire into the air, the air into ether or space, and that into the Tāmasic aspect of Ego (which is the source of all elements), the Tāmasic ego into the mahat (the principle of cosmic intelligence), but of the intellectual and objective aspects of the Ātman, he contemplated the intellectual or knowledge aspect into the Mahat ignoring the objective aspect, and thereby burnt down completely the Nescience (that obscured the soul). Through his intelligent consciousness of the absolute bliss of Nirvāṇa, he transcended that aspect also, and completely severed off the bondage (such as identification of the soul with the body), and stood in his own essential nature which is beyond the range of description, and abstract reasoning.
[back to top]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
The gists of the explanations of different commentators are as follows:
(I) Bhāgavata Candrikā: Hariścandra in his meditation identified the mind with the earth and the earth with water... so on up to Tāmasic Ego into Pradhāna which contains Mahat in itself. Having contemplated the Intelligent principle in it (i.e. by contemplating the pratyagātman as being characterised by devotion-cum-knowledge—upāsanātmaka-vidyā-viśiṣṭam pratyagātmānam anusandhāya ityarthaḥ /), and burning down by that knowledge the wrong notions of the identification of the body with the soul, absolute independence of ātman (svatantrātmā'bhimāna-rūpam), and discarding it by realising the limitations of that aspect of devotion-cum-knowledge, and becoming free from the bondage of karmas (merits and sins), he realised his blissful nature of the soul which is beyond description (either as a man or a god or a sub-human being etc. or in usual terms of name and form), stayed in his own essential nature (transcend sin etc.).
(II) Padaratnāvalī: Agreeing with Bhāvāratha Dīpikā about the procedure of merging of the mind into the earth etc. upto Mahal which is presided over by the four- faced god Brahmā, wherein lies the Intelligent principle of which the presiding deity is Sarasvatī; contemplating over this and destroying all the bondages, he stayed in his natural stage of knowledge and blissfulness which is beyond my i.e. human power of description or reasoning capacity.
(III) Siddhāntapradīpa: The fickleness of the mind depends on the food eaten. Thus the mind being identical with the food which grows out of the earth and hence one with it for the control of the mind, he meditated it as identical with the earth. This procedure goes upto Mahadātman: but Siddhāntapradīpa states mahadātman means Pradhāna which is the soul or cause of Mahat. Jñānakalā=Buddhi, ‘Intelligence’. Though it is jaḍa (unconscious), it is associated with the knower i.e. Jīva and hence called Jñānakalā. Contemplating over the distinction between jīvātman and buddhi and burning down (dispelling) the ignorance i.e. the false identification of the soul with body, mind, intelligence etc., and thus discarding intelligence and distinguishing Pradhāna from the self, he became liberated from bondage of karmas.
(IV) Bhaktamanorañjanī takes jñānakalā as the power of knowledge of jīva (jñāna- śabda-vācya-jīvasya vidyā-śakti / Hence—upāsanāviśiṣṭa-pratyagātma.

Footnote [5]:
mano-mūlo hi saṃsāraḥ! Śruti text quoted by Bhāvāratha Dīpikā and Anvitārthaprakāśikā.

Footnote [6]:
anna-mayāṃ he saumya manaḥ!—Chāndogya up. 6.6.5 6.7.6.—quoted by commentators.

devanagari: विश्वामित्रो भृशं प्रीतो ददौ अविहतां गतिम् । मनः पृथिव्यां तामद्भिः तेजसापोऽनिलेन तत् ॥ २५ ॥
